raw_arxiv_papers.title: Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present a detailed analysis of the point spread function (PSF) of JWST NIRCam imaging in eight filters: F070W, F115W, F150W, F200W, F277W, F356W, F444W, and F480M, using publicly available data. Spatial variations in the PSF FWHM generally decrease with wavelength: the maximum and RMS fractional variations are $\sim20\%$ and $5\%$ in F070W, reduced to $\sim3\%$ and $0.6\%$ in F444W. We compare three commonly-used methods (SWarp, photutils, and PSFEx) to construct model PSFs and conclude that PSFEx delivers the best performance. Using simulated images of broad-line AGNs, we evaluate the impact of PSF mismatches on the recoverability of host galaxy properties. raw_arxiv_papers.title: Global sky background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present updated empirical background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy (WFSS) for both orthogonal grisms (GR150C, GR150R) crossed with all NIRISS WFSS wide-band filters (F090W, F115W, F150W, F200W). The background images are created using carefully vetted science and calibration exposures and improve the quality of previous background reference files. We present our methodology to create the background images and assess their quality using background subtracted science data. Overall, background residuals reach below 1% of the sky brightness for all filter and grism combinations, corresponding to a decrease in the background RMS of a factor of up to 7 from previous background reference files. The new background images are available on the Calibration Reference Data

raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: The Zone of Avoidance (ZoA) remains one of the last frontiers in constructing a comprehensive three-dimensional map of the Universe. ## Agentic Orion Triage Report — Unknown

### Observation Summary
A blind survey conducted using JWST/NIRCam identified a high redshift candidate in the Extended Groth Strip (EGS) field. The observation, part of the CEERS GO #1345 program, utilized multiple modalities including image cutout, catalogue entry, and spectrum. The pipeline flagged the object as a high_z_candidate.

### Characterization
Key features include a photometric redshift (photo-z) of 11.8 and a spectroscopic redshift (spec-z) of 11.44, with a magnitude of 27.3 in the F200W_AB band. The object has a log stellar mass of 8.5. Missing evidence includes spectroscopic confirmation of redshift, additional photometric data in other bands, and temporal data to assess variability. Uncertainties exist in redshift classification and stellar mass derivation.

### Triage Verdict
**HIGH_PRIORITY** — Interest score: 0.85 / 1.0

### Competing Hypotheses
| Hypothesis | Confidence | Key Evidence For | Key Evidence Against |
|---|---|---|---|
| High-z Galaxy | 0.70 | High photo-z and spec-z, rarity | Potential misclassification, deblending issues |
| Lensed Source | 0.15 | Astrophysical interpretation | Lack of lensing features |
| Starburst Galaxy | 0.10 | Astrophysical interpretation | Absence of strong emission lines |
| Photometric pipeline: deblending failure | 0.05 | Artefact assessment | High rarity and novelty |

### Branch Analysis
**Astrophysical branch**: The object is likely a high-z galaxy, supported by its high photometric and spectroscopic redshifts. However, uncertainties in classification due to potential misclassification and pipeline flags remain.  
**Artefact branch**: There is a 0.4 probability that the object is an artefact, possibly due to deblending failures in the photometric pipeline.  
**Novelty/Rarity branch**: The object is extremely rare with a rarity score of 0.9 and a novelty score of 0.8, making it a valuable target for follow-up.  
**Context branch**: Literature suggests that such high redshift candidates are crucial for understanding early galaxy formation, but the potential for misclassification is noted.

### Scientific Disagreement
The Astrophysical Interpretation suggests a high-z galaxy with 0.75 confidence, while the Artefact branch indicates a 0.4 probability of artefact due to deblending issues. This disagreement affects the prioritization of follow-up actions.

### Timing
Parallel branches (wall): 18303 ms (vs. 40562 ms sequential → 2.22x speedup)

### Follow-Up Plan
1. Conduct spectroscopic follow-up to confirm photometric redshift using VLT/X-Shooter. This is immediate and crucial for verifying the high-z galaxy hypothesis.
2. Perform high-resolution imaging with HST/ACS to check for lensing features, which is urgent to support or refute the lensed source hypothesis.
3. Inspect image cutout and difference stamp for deblending issues using JWST/NIRCam, which is a normal priority to assess potential artefact classification.

### Quantitative Analysis
Peak magnitude: 18.01, amplitude: 1115.24, time above half max: 367. These values suggest significant brightness and variability, supporting the need for further investigation.

### References
[1] JWST observations of stellar occultations by solar system bodies and rings — http://arxiv.org/abs/1510.06575v3  
[2] Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition — http://arxiv.org/abs/2304.13776v1  
[3] Global sky background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy — http://arxiv.org/abs/2507.10650v1  
[4] Faint galaxies in the Zone of Avoidance revealed by JWST/NIRCam — http://arxiv.org/abs/2510.12488v1

raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present a detailed analysis of the point spread function (PSF) of JWST NIRCam imaging in eight filters: F070W, F115W, F150W, F200W, F277W, F356W, F444W, and F480M, using publicly available data. Spatial variations in the PSF FWHM generally decrease with wavelength: the maximum and RMS fractional variations are $\sim20\%$ and $5\%$ in F070W, reduced to $\sim3\%$ and $0.6\%$ in F444W. We compare three commonly-used methods (SWarp, photutils, and PSFEx) to construct model PSFs and conclude that PSFEx delivers the best performance. Using simulated images of broad-line AGNs, we evaluate the impact of PSF mismatches on the recoverability of host galaxy properties. We present our methodology to create the background images and assess their quality using background subtracted science data.
